text.content:
Caelith's Conclusion — Read-Aloud

Read aloud at the start of Scene 7, on the third morning. The evidence is still on the long table; the city is going about its day outside. Caelith judges the party ready to choose how they leave.

Caelith sets the copied countermark beside the clipped coin.

"Cindren and Vhal," he says. "Bonded factors. Respectable enough for clean rooms. Useful enough for dirty ones." He pins the eastern coast to a small map between the squares of linen. "Their house is in Rel Astra, the Old Harbor Ward. The senior partners answer to no one in this kingdom or the next, in any way the law would let us reach them."

Outside, bells ring.

"I do not know whether Haskur Vandrell is there. But someone there was ready for him." He looks at each of you in turn. "And if we have learned this much by morning — then by nightfall they will know we learned it."

Transitions to: Scene 7 · Three Roads East (next page). Caelith now calls the party up to the Old Council Chamber and lays three leather folios on the round table.

GM note. This is the speech that crosses the line. Until now the party has been investigating at home; with this read-aloud, the question pivots from "what happened?" to "how soon do we leave?"

Scene 7 · Three Roads East

The Old Council Chamber. Three folios. Authority, cover, or distance.

After Caelith's conclusion in the Refectory, he calls the party up to the cold, disused Old Council Chamber, where Trina is already seated among the ring of carved chairs. He lays three leather folios on the round table. Three ways east, and — as he says it himself, a finger on each in turn — "I can give you authority, cover, or distance. I cannot give you all three."

Before the folios hit the table — the gate check

Run your gate check first: the five truths from the front of the chapter. The party should leave Loftwick certain that Haskur was kept alive on purpose, that he was a node in a network, that the firm's living hand is in Hardby, that Loftwick won't let them finish here, and that the road runs east and must be taken now. If any of the five is thin, the Quick reference tells you which late line shores it up. Then let them choose their road — and make sure they feel the weight of it, because this choice shapes the next chapter. Speed may save Tamsin; visibility may get her "settled."

The Official Delegation (authority)

The party travels as accredited Yeomanry representatives, with credentials, a junior councilman to lend his name, clerks, and a guard escort. It is slow — the speed of paperwork, with a formal stop and a written report at every customs post — and visible: the firm will know they're coming and exactly when, with all the time in the world to sanitize records and move witnesses. But Aerdi authorities can't touch accredited foreign inquirers, the firm is bound by law to receive them, and any witness they recover can travel home under diplomatic cover.

Filing the credentials advances the Pressure Clock two segments at once, and one more per week of travel — the loudest road.

Commercial Cover (cover — Caelith's quiet recommendation)

The party travels as a small wool-buying delegation assessing eastern grain markets, with real samples, real letters of introduction, and Trina's letter to the poet Castrian Vell, who keeps an ear in four Hardby salons. Faster than the delegation, and it buys plausible access — wool merchants visit counting houses for credit all the time — but it demands discipline: anyone who breaks character risks the group, and a paladin in plate or a gnome bard known to the Hardby tavern circuit thins the cover fast.

It doesn't advance the clock at departure; it advances only if the cover seriously breaks.

Quiet Pursuit (distance)

Fresh horses, light packs, false papers, no official capacity. Fastest by a wide margin, and the only road that surprises the firm — they won't know the party is moving until it arrives in Hardby. But there's no net: caught east of Hardby with false papers, the party are just liars to the local watch, and Caelith says it plainly — "If you are taken, I cannot reach you."

It doesn't advance the clock at departure either, unless the party is recognized in Hardby and can't slip away.

A fourth idea

Caelith will hear a fourth idea if the party has one — sea passage from Saltmarsh (slower, but it bypasses Hardby entirely), their own teleport magic (he won't provide one and is wary of the Aerdi reading magical arrivals) — and he'll advise against splitting up: "You are stronger than my agents because you are together."

Trina, asked, says nothing about the first or third roads; of the second she says only, "Castrian's house is mine in everything but the address. He will know you are mine. Do not embarrass him."

Hand-off to the next chapter

The road the party chose sets the conditions the next chapter opens under. Price the choice; don't editorialize it — none of these is a failure.

Road takenTime the firm has hadTamsin Moraven, in HardbyTheir Hardby contacts
Official Delegation (authority)~2 weeksConfirmed dead — a body recovered days before they arrive.Witnesses scattered; the broker moved upcountry; a paper trail only.
Commercial Cover (cover)~1 weekAlive, moved to a coastal estate two days east — recoverable with effort.Veska Maelan and Castrian Vell both reachable; the broker confrontable.
Quiet Pursuit (distance)~3 daysAlive, still in Hardby — held in a warehouse, a live rescue.Every contact unprepared and within reach; the broker easiest of all.

The Pressure Clock — Cross-Reference

Six segments on a notecard where everyone can see them. Advance it when the conspiracy can see the party act (showing their hand at the firm; rattling Pollow; a public spectacle; the Sarth night) and when a whole day passes with nothing done. Don't advance it for quiet, honest work. Aim to reach Scene 7 at three or four segments — pressure landing on the party without ever having stopped them.

The full segment-by-segment table, the trigger list, and the "if it runs hot / if it runs cold" guidance live on the Quick Reference journal: @UUID[Compendium.blooming-rot-2.phase-2-journals.JournalEntry.BR2P2QRefJournal.JournalEntryPage.BR2P2QrPgClock]{The Pressure Clock — full reference}. Run from that page.

Ending Read-Aloud — Departing East

Read aloud at the moment the party leaves Loftwick under whichever travel approach they chose. The text below is approach-agnostic — it works whether they ride out at noon as a delegation or before dawn as nameless travelers. Adjust the small details (number of horses, presence of clerks, the form of farewell) to match.

Loftwick is at its quietest in the hour you leave. The Civic Way is broad and pale; the lamps along the Audit Hall still burn though the sky has gone the color of new milk. At the Little Palace gate the footmen raise their lanterns once and lower them — the old Yeomanry sign for safe travel.

Caelith stands at the gate. He embraces no one. He steps to the lead rider and offers a small flat brass token — the quill above the closed gate. "If you find the man who underwrote him, write to me at this house, with this on the seal. Three days from now I will be in this office, or its successor. The token will reach me either way."

Trina is at his shoulder. She has already said what she needed to say, last night, in private, to the last of you she walked with. The silver leaf at her collar catches the early light. She lifts a hand, and lowers it.

The road bends out of the Civic Way onto the Wool Road, and by the time the walls drop behind you the lamps of the Audit Hall have gone out. You ride an hour before anyone speaks, and when someone finally does, you find that none of you has yet asked the question you all carry: what was waiting for him in Rel Astra — and is it waiting still.

Where the next chapter picks up

Hardby is six days east if they press the horses. Rel Astra is eleven on the map, and, on Caelith's quiet reckoning, closer than that — he hasn't said by how much. The next chapter opens at the gates of Hardby, whichever road they took.
